summary: Round 5 closes the original pill-over-button path, but it does not fully resolve the consumer-side ARIA case. Compact `r9-aria-role` controls no longer win as `clickable/pill`, yet they can still fall through to `clickable/link` because the link scorer gets an ARIA bonus while the button scorer has no matching ARIA path. The new regression test also only asserts “not pill”, so it would not catch that misclassification. I would keep this at `warn` until button-like ARIA controls are forced or at least preferentially routed to `clickable/button`, or the producer emits finer ARIA role detail and/

issues.description: The new cap prevents `clickable/pill` from winning for compact `r9-aria-role` controls, but it does not make those controls resolve to `clickable/button`. For the stated regression shape (`detection='r9-aria-role'`, label `Save`, bounds `60x28`), pill is capped to 40, `clickable/button` scores only 60, and `clickable/link` scores 80 because it adds both the ARIA-role bonus and the compact-height /
issues.suggested_fix: Either add a consumer-side button preference for compact short-label `r9-aria-role` controls, or remove the unconditional ARIA bonus from `clickable/link` and reintroduce ARIA role handling only when the producer distinguishes `role=button` from `role=link/tab`.

issues.description: The new regression test asserts only that the ARIA case is not classified as `clickable/pill`. That allows the same sample to regress to `clickable/link`, which is exactly what the current scoring implies.
issues.suggested_fix: Strengthen the regression to assert the expected winning pattern for the current intended behavior. If the intended behavior is still ambiguous until producer work lands, add an explicit comment and a temporary assertion on score ordering so the gap stays visible.

strengths: The pill heuristic is materially tighter than the original version: short alpha labels now require tight geometry, which addresses the earlier nav-label false positives.
The button-leaf cap remains a sound consumer-side safeguard for real `<button>` selectors.
The trainer/test separation and the accumulated regression coverage look substantially better than the starting state.
